transcript.text: Hey, how are you?
I'm doing good, thanks.
How about you?
It's great to meet you.
Yeah, likewise.
Where are you based again?
I'm in Los Angeles.
Oh, nice.
You're not too far away.
How's that light?
it warm?
It warmed up here a little.
Yeah, there's supposed to be a heat wave later this week, but we'll see.
We'll see.
So I've just been staying indoors.
Yeah, nice, nice.
Are you originally from SoCal?
Yeah, I was born and raised in LA.
left for a few years to go to university up at UC Davis, so close to Sacramento.
Yeah, and then moved back after.
You stayed west coast most of your life, I think?
Yeah.
That's cool.
Well, so nice to connect.
I've heard so many good things, and the team's been really excited to chat with you.
How has it been, the whole experience so far?
Yeah, I really enjoyed the interview experience.
I feel like I've had so many opportunities to learn more about the company and what the role is like.
And to really.
Hear about how just working at GrowthX in general is an opportunity to, you know, build something very early on, just something that I'm personally really excited about.
Yeah, so it's been a good process overall, and I've appreciated Marilia being like very communicative, answering my questions.
So that's been nice.
Yeah, that's awesome.
Well, for digging in, I always like to start with maybe me answering questions for you, since we've done a lot of asking questions of you.
Anything that has been like kind of top of mind or that I can add some color or light to?
Yeah, I think the thing I'm most curious about, which I've talked about a little bit with Andy and Matthew, is just the long-term vision for GrowthX.
I understand that, you know, you've built your own platform and kind of the vision is to eventually have an external version that customers can access.
But I'm curious as to what that looks like or what else you're kind of imagining for the future of the company.
Yeah, yeah, absolutely.
So the way I kind of think about it, we have a...
A couple of different layers, if you will.
So the service layer around content itself right now, it is really like the perfect wedge for what we're doing.
And I'll kind of explain a little bit.
It just so happens that content, as you probably know, has been, you know, I've been a CMO for so many years, been at so many companies, built content programs, all those companies.
And every single time, there was really not a good tool.
I remember early day, I was literally taking, you know, different CMSs, like WordPress and whatnot, trying to customize it or building things on top of CMSs.
And then after that, was like, okay, let me buy a tool like Kaposta back in the day or, you know, workflow automation tools for content.
And then later on, it was like, okay, let me build it all in Airtable and then try to stitch it all together.
So like content has always been a little bit of kind of this forgotten thing.
You know, CMS has gotten all this investment.
There's all these big players in CMS, you know, marketing automation has.
Massive players, then like content itself is just like, yeah, whatever, just use an editor.
Like, you know, use a Google doc.
duct tape it together.
Yeah, figure it out.
Yeah, you know, just use a spreadsheet to keep tab of everything and good luck with everything else, you know?
And then copy and paste it in the CMS, why not?
You know, like, and so like, that's just a pain I think that we're solving.
That's something that I've always experienced.
It just so happens that like, we wanted to do the service and we weren't setting out to build like a tech company or whatever.
I just started building AI workflows and in the process of doing that, we just kept breaking everything and just realizing there was nothing out there that we could use.
So eventually what we realized is that coding agents are just getting better and better and they're only going to keep getting better.
And so why are tools trying to abstract cold away with like drag and drop tools?
And so what we built was a coding agent that enables us to just build workflows a lot faster, you know?
Right, yeah.
And them at a much bigger scale.
And then we realized, okay, we are not trying to replace experts.
We're trying.
To augment their thought process and what they do, but there's no platform to do the kind of this orchestration between AI workflows, especially agentic workflows and experts.
so, for instance, like when a workflow needs intervention or needs help from an expert or is ready for review, like there's nothing to orchestrate that.
And really, like, it's not just about saying, hey, Nathalie, here you go.
Here's a piece of content.
Make it better.
It's really about, like, what is the context?
What do you want the expert to do?
You know, and how are you going to evaluate that it's done?
And how can you help that person actually learn as part of the process as well?
And like, by the way, you might want to think about this, this, and this.
These are things that you've done in the past, or these are things that this client has valued in the past, right?
And over time, every one of those interactions is an opportunity for the platform as a whole to learn.
And so we have kind of like two principles.
One is, how do we, as models get better, everything we do should get better, faster, cheaper, and faster, you know, and then this.
Second thing is, as with every run and everything we deliver and do, we should be getting better as a whole as well.
And if those two things are true, then we're doing our job and we're building the platform the right way.
And so to go back to your question, I know that was the long version, is like we were building these kind of common cores that we just need for ourselves.
But then it's turning out that a lot of these common cores are things that there are multiple companies that need it.

You know, so right now, we, this week, we just built a custom knowledge base that you can say, like, ingest your entire site map, or you might ingest like, like we have like a healthcare customer that might have very specific, like, like research papers that they want store, you know, in order.
Right.
Yeah.
And an assistant that allows you to kind of like, almost kind of like vibe code content in some ways.
Like there's a lot of these things that we're building that a lot of people want that directly.
And so we're thinking through exposing, it's just that right now, the let's call it.
ContentOS part, which is like truly like the platform that we're doing most of the work is like supporting this content motion and is what a lot of people are willing to pay a lot of money for.
And there's a lot of value in that.
And so like we're trying to stay focused, you know, and then over time, like if there's pieces that we can expose or that we can productize or we can sell the API usage alone, then we can do that.
Right.
Yeah.
Okay.
Yeah.
You know, that totally makes sense.

And even just talking about like the custom knowledge base that you just so that you get you all created, I feel like that's something that I've kind of just had to do manually a lot of the time over and over again for multiple clients.
And just kind of like you kind of mentioned before, we're like plugging a bunch of things together like it's in Google Docs, but also you're building like projects and whatever AI tool you're using.
And then just, I think having that kind of custom tool that's not that's inherently built for that, it sounds like.
It's a great, you know, problem solver for your clients.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
And I guess like for you, right?
Like what's probably like the most exciting or energizing part about potentially this role?
You know, I know we've reached out, but yeah.

Yeah, I think, I mean, I kind of mentioned a little bit earlier that I just in general, getting to work at an early stage company, just the opportunity to build things early on and really have an impact on a wider scale and get to work with other people who are also excited about that as well.
I think it's just, I mean, to use your word, very energizing.
It's inspiring to work with people like that.

And then also I think it's, GrowthX offers a really interesting intersection of, of course there's, you know, the service aspect, like content marketing, content strategies, things for clients, but also the product aspect as well, which is something that I've been looking for in my next role.
It's not only to provide services, but to get to help build something that will last beyond what I do, or that can continue without my...
You know, constant input or work.
I think that's the thing that's the most exciting to me is really getting to participate and collaborate with others and build something together.
Yeah, that makes sense.
And so like, one thing that I always curious about, like, when you're doing work for a customer, right?
Like, how do you approach it?
Like, let's say you start with a new customer tomorrow, right?
Like, what's the mindset?

Or maybe give an example of, you know, a customer that you've recently took on, or even if it's not super recent, just always curious how people approach it, especially when at the beginning where they're kind of having to learn context and build relationships with people and earn their trust a little bit, you know?
Yeah, that's something I've done several times during my time at Animals and actually fairly recently.
We've onboarded a client that I've worked from from the beginning.
So I think something that's really helpful is to start out with some sort of survey or doc where they can, we can ask them questions and they can answer them.
very for me.
For example, like, what are some brands whose content you admire?
What are your content goals?
Can you give us an example of the content you've produced that you really like, that you'd like to do more of?
Even just, like, sharing, you know, typical docs that would be helpful.
So, like, messaging, if they have brand guidelines, if they have any, like, sales docs that they can share with us, battle cards, competitor analysis, that kind of thing.

And trying to get that information early on so that as I'm conducting my research into the company before we have our first, you know, official kickoff call at the beginning of the engagement, I have as much context as possible so that I can ask, you know, smart questions and make sure that our first meeting is as productive as possible, rather than me asking questions that could have been answered if I just had asked prior for some resources.
So, once we have the kickoff call, that's where I, you know, I do have, like, a structure that I follow, but also tailoring it to the client.
So, for example, some clients maybe don't have a lot of messaging or brand guidelines or anything in the beginning.

So maybe I would ask more questions about what do you want your brand to sound like, or what are your goals, especially if they have one of my clients right now, when we first started with them, they had zero marketing, all of their, they were just like outbound sales.
had no like inbound marketing, anything like that.
So for them, it was really like starting from scratch, like building out their brand voice, what types of content or marketing would be so for them, and then even expanding beyond content.

But just to get back on track of like working from a client from the beginning, we have the kickoff call, I asked questions about maybe some of the problems that they're facing, also asking like, why did you choose to engage an agency, maybe they have an internal team, but they're spread thin, or they only have like a one person team, and it's not possible for them to do everything that they want.

Or there are some teams, some clients I've had that they have an internal marketing team, but they just need help scaling content, or they want input on a specific type of content strategy, or working with a specific target audience or something.
So from there, we have the kickoff call.

And then that's when I have more context and I'm able to really feel confident that I understand what they're looking for, what their goals are, some of the challenges that they faced, and also some more information about where they stand compared to their competitors.
And from there is when I start to go into like the research aspect.

So audits, competitive gap analysis, just auditing their website performance in general, like a content refresh analysis, looking at what they've published and seeing what are the things that we should refresh based on, you know, potential for not just traffic, although sometimes maybe that's the only metric we have to work with, but ideally revenue.
Like what are things that drive revenue that we can focus on?
And really, I think something that I like to prioritize in my content strategy is things that help them prioritize their content roadmap.
So it's one thing to come up with like a hundred different ideas and then hand it to them in a spreadsheet and then they don't know where to go from there.
So that's something that I really try to keep in mind as I'm doing all of these audits.
And then something new that...
What you've been doing the past couple months is also having an AI, AI search visibility analysis.

So how do our clients show up in like chat GPT, AI overviews, perplexity, things like that, compared to their competitors and surface opportunities where they can refresh their content so that they're appearing in places where they aren't, especially if it's places where their competitors are and they should be showing up.

And then also maybe coming up with ideas for new content that they should create, especially if it's something that's like, like you can duplicate this type of content across multiple topics or content pillars, things like that.
And I would say that's like the early stage of the engagement and overall like the audit and strategy process is like that.

At the end of the, or at the end of like this whole process, we have a presentation where we present, I present the strategy, explain the findings in our audits and explain like our tactics or the tactics that I've built based on my findings and then a content roadmap for them.
And then of course,
Just taking in feedback during a presentation because they know their industry, their audience, they know everything better than I do, no matter how much research or auditing I do.
So I'm always really open to feedback and wanting to collaborate with clients as much as possible.
Sometimes they don't want that or they don't have the time for that.
And they really want me or my team to just be the experts and take the lead, which, of course, you have to be flexible on what people want.
But I just find that being able to collaborate deeply because I'm not embedded in their routine.
There's no way I'm going to know everything the way they do.
So I really, especially in the beginning, want to have as much collaboration and feedback as possible.

And once we build that trust or I gain more familiarity or even there's some times where I've become really knowledgeable about some very niche topics, they feel more comfortable kind of like, oh, we don't need to review everything.
We just review the final drop.
And like, okay, so if you think about your favorite customer right now.
How would that customer describe you?
I think my favorite customer right now, it's kind of ironic because it's not, they're not the easiest customer to work with because we just like to be so flexible with them.
I kind of mentioned earlier as an example, they're the customer that had like zero marketing in the beginning.
So we basically built their marketing from the beginning.
I think it's been almost a year since we started working with them.

And I would say in working with them, they've mentioned multiple times that they appreciate that we're like really flexible with, you know, changing what we're doing or maybe experimenting with other things.
Typically, of course, like our services are more content marketing focused.
But for the past few months, we've been working on a campaign to help them collect reviews on G2, which is not like typical content marketing, but a lot of it is applicable.
Like you're building email campaigns or building surveys to help, you know, collect NPS scores, that kind of thing.
So there is a little bit of transferable skills there, but it's definitely like outside.

My wheelhouse, not something I'd ever done before, but it was clear that that's something they needed, and so they really appreciated that, even though that's not exactly what they hired us for, what we do, that we were able to, you know, figure it out, like, look into what works best and build, like, a review incentive program and build, like, these surveys and this process for collecting reviews and reengaging people from their customer base.

So I would say that, I think, is a very solid and recent example of a customer that I really enjoyed working with, and I think they really appreciate that we're always there, kind of, like, closing that gap for them or figuring out where we can help them.
I love that.
And then, like, for, do you think about, like, kind of the day-to-day for a lot of what you're doing?
What do you think are the things that really energize you?
And then what are some things that drain you that you wish you didn't have to do as much of or at all?
Yeah, I think.
Both my answer to both of those questions comes from the fact that essentially, I'm like a full stack content marketer.

So I do everything from like, the kickoff and the research and the strategy and audits to content production, and then also distribution and reporting, like everything from beginning to end, like cycle over and over again.

So I would say the thing that I get most energized by is getting to meet with clients, getting to, you know, get to know them on a personal, like build that relationship level, but also get to really understand what their company is doing, what's happening internally, like with their product or services or whatever they're doing, what's happening in their industry at large.

They're so, you know, in their industry that they have a lot of really interesting ideas or just insights that maybe I wouldn't know just from like, you know, being on LinkedIn or researching online and Reddit or something.
And so getting to work closely with clients and collaborate and also just collaborate with people on my team in general, which sometimes can be a little tricky with like a globally distributed team.
Sometimes like my 9 a.m.
like the end of someone's day.

So any opportunity I have to meet or meet with someone on Zoom or collaborate that way is something that I really, I feel like I just am more productive that way rather than trying to like figure out and problem solve on my own all the time.
If I can have like another perspective to work with, that really helps me.

And then something that I think is like draining is just having to do, again, like all that full stack content marketing all at once for all my clients all the time, especially content production and then switching to like strategy or reporting or distribution.

The context switching alone is like really mentally draining, which I think is something that really appeals to me about this role with GrowthX is not having to be responsible for like the full life cycle of content marketing and getting to really focus on certain areas where I think just having the time to focus on those parts, like improving workflows, reviewing content.
And really, like, having that editorial judgment in general, I think just will help me make a greater impact on the team.
Yeah, that's perfect.

And, like, what, from a, just an editorial perspective, right, like, especially, like, content quality, how do you think about that, like, content quality and what makes some piece of content, especially, like, more technical or more nuanced piece of content, really high quality?
Or how do you approach that challenge of something over-delivering on expectations for the reader, you know?
Yeah, I think that is definitely my priority when it comes to content.
Like, of course, when we're creating content, we have to think about, like, algorithms and now AI search, all these things.
But I think the top priority is to make content useful and valuable to human readers.
Because people can see right through something if they know you're, like, stuffing a piece with a bunch of keywords for SEO purposes or something.
Like, it's awkward and inorganic.
Like,
But I would say that to like summarize it, I think high quality content needs to be specific, helpful, and actionable.
So let's say you have like a comprehensive guide on something.
don't think that's necessarily always the best way to approach content, but a comprehensive guide should definitely include like concrete next steps on what you should do after reading this guide.
Like it shouldn't just provide like a vague, like, oh, what are the benefits and challenges of this topic?
Or something I've been working on recently with a client is like how to build an online marketplace.
So there's benefits and challenges to that type of business model.
That's all well and good, but how do you like apply those benefits and challenges to the process of building a marketplace?
So ideally that content should have really specific actionable recommendations that people know exactly what they can do.
And it should include, you know, real world examples of like other companies that have done this or if it's a company.

I mean, if a client, you have an opportunity to talk to them, get more insights into, like, their methodology or how they have helped other customers do things, get that, like, unique perspective and incorporate them into the content so that this is, you know, information that the reader can't get elsewhere.
But also, they don't have to, like, go around researching on their own.
Like, okay, now I know how to, let's say, build, like, the minimum viable product of something.
What should that include?
What is a minimum viable product?
Why should I be building that instead of trying to build, like, the perfect version of my product from the beginning?
Things like that.
trying to make it easier for the reader so they don't have to, like, if they want to go down a rabbit hole later, they can, but that's not required of them in content.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Is there, like, a time when a piece of content was extremely challenging and you couldn't get to a point where the customer is really happy and eventually you figure it out?
Or maybe it didn't in, like, you know, decided.
know, maybe
Do something else?
Do you mean like they're not satisfied with the quality or direction?
like you had to kind of keep at it because like it wasn't good enough for a customer.
Like we've had a couple of customers here and there where, you know, even our best people are like, this is good.
And then the customer's like, yeah, no, not there.
Like, you're just like.
Yeah.
Yeah, no, that's definitely, I think that can be just the tricky part with working with clients in general is sometimes they don't know what they don't want until they get something.
So I think especially that's something I've come across a lot in like the earliest phase of an engagement or really just kind of like trying to understand each other.
And so I would say, what's an example?
I've worked with some of my clients for a while now.
So it's not as like recent in my mind where I've had this process.
I think an example would be for the startup client that I mentioned where we built their marketing from the ground up.

In the beginning, they, I think, had a lot of, on the one hand, they wanted us to kind of, you know, like, be independent and do things on our own, but also wanting to review the content at the end, which, of course, is part of our process no matter what.
And then having a lot of input on, oh, we need to focus on these aspects or this isn't really helpful to our audience, maybe talk about it this way or cut this part out.

And I think, for me, in general, I'm just always open to any kind of feedback, positive or negative, so that's really helpful for me, rather than just trying to guess, especially at the beginning when we're still getting to know each other, I'm still learning about their industry, their readers, that kind of thing.

So, I would say, for me, like, how I, like, approach that challenge is just always letting people know that I'm open to feedback and explaining how, like, the revision process would work or also trying to limit maybe how many people are reviewing something, like, if you suddenly have, like, people.
Something that is just naturally going to slow down the process.
Yeah.
I don't know if that super answers your question.
Yeah.
Well, I appreciate it.
And I know we're going have a ton of time, but I'm happy to answer any additional questions or go through anything that you think would be helpful for you as well.
Yeah.
I guess I am also curious.
I know GrowthX is like pretty early stage startup and I'm curious what kind of like flexibility is there to explore like other types of roles or responsibilities within the company.
So I know this is like a technical managing editor role, but if I were interested in things like strategy or operations, these are just examples.
Yeah.
What kind of opportunities are there to explore?
Yeah.
think a better answer to that is giving examples rather than saying, yes, there's opportunities, you know?
So for instance, I don't know if you ever cross.
I think it was before your time at Animals at Aida.
Yeah, yeah.
Aida and I were on the same team.
And I actually, I think I spoke to her a couple of weeks ago, actually.
Yeah, so she's amazing.
She's been promoted already.
She's been here less than six months and she's leading all our strategist sprints.
And she's freaking amazing.
I think we've promoted her technically twice now in six months.
And, you know, keep giving her more responsibilities and different things.
And, you know, and she's awesome, super positive, and she's the best.
know, Andy, who's our head of customer operations, like she joined two months ago, three months ago.
And now a lot of the directors report to her and she like is killing it.
And, you know, is now part of the leadership team, you know, and it's like two, three months, you know.
Let's see what other examples.
George joined and was leading some of the client kickoffs and things like that.
And he was killing it and has been killing it.
And recently I promoted him to my Chief SF.
And let's see.
Yeah, like, I think there's like the four managing editors that join in December, all four of them got promoted already.
And so there's like a ton of opportunity and there's not a lack of things that need help.
Yeah, of course.
so I think the, I will counter that also with the other side, which is like the bar is high.

I think we have a really kind culture and, you know, like what we're trying to do is make sure that we do the best work of our lives, that we inspire everyone around us and outside, and that we do more than we think was possible before.
And if those three things are there, then, you know, we're going to be in a really good place.
But I think also like the

A few people that didn't work out or were probably a little like too focused on the wrong things as well at the beginning, you know, like it's not necessarily like they didn't know what they were doing.

It's mostly like, you know, not focused on the customer outcomes and, and like being resourceful and delivering and, you know, kind of being, having their hands on, figuring out what great looks like before trying to delegate, like, like just the foundational of that, like we're all like my co-founder and I, like we're hands-on, we're doing the things.
Like I take out the trash in the office, know what I mean?
Like, like, it's not like no one is above anything.
And so as long as people come with that mentality, then they grow really fast and they succeed really fast.

It's the people that don't that, that like sometimes might be too distracted with like titles or, you know, the next thing and not focusing on, you know, doing what's best for the company and for the customers now, you know?
Yeah, I can totally understand that.
I think that makes a lot of sense.
I mean, especially in an early stage startup, like, right, the stereotype is you wear a lot of hats.
So.
I that makes a lot of sense, yeah.
Well, happy to answer if there's another question or not.
I know we're out of time, but it's been really lovely talking and excited.
Yeah, I do have one question.
I'm curious if I can ask about what is the runway for the company?
Yeah, yeah.
We have a little over $10 million in the bank right now, so we barely touched our Series A.
And we have well over almost two years of runway, but our growth is going down each week.
I mean, each month.
And so we're on track to be profitable again by the end of the year.
But also we've gotten over 40 inbounds from investors just in the last two months.
Okay, wow.
A very good chance we, you know, if we wanted to raise, we can raise like tomorrow.
And so- Wow, that's so exciting.
Yeah, yeah.
And we're getting, we're two contracts away from hitting $10 million in runway, I mean, run rate and on track to close the year, probably closer to $15 million.
In revenue, which means pretty much nothing to 15 in pretty much a year.
So it's pretty, pretty exciting.
And, you know, we also had some amazing logos start, like, I think, which is really exciting.
Like, like Sentinel One of normal security, like Webflow, you know, like just like a lot of really amazing logos.
And so it's like every day you are having meetings with these companies.
It's like, this is crazy.
These are like the best companies out there, you know, are really established companies.
And so there's a lot of work to do as well, though.
So I don't want to make it seem like it's all, all easy.
Yeah, I mean, of course, of course.
There's a lot of  to figure out and, and in order to keep customers happy as well, you know.
Yeah, and that's what makes it rewarding, of course, I imagine.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
But I think the company is doing really well.
And, and we have amazing people and, but also a lot of stuff to figure out together.
Yeah, I think that's something that I find exciting is just being able to, you know, start early rather than trying to fix things later.
on after things have been kind of like going for many years, trying to reverse them or put things back together in a better way.
So, yeah.
Well, it's been lovely talking.
We're moving super fast on our end.
So I appreciate you taking the time and being flexible with the process.
So it's been really great.
Yeah, it was really great to meet you, too, Marcel.
I appreciate it.
Yeah, likewise.
Talk soon.
Thanks.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interview with Nathalie Schrans for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/JUt1q6VQCTbYYehU8KF8pr_SDpsxzxft?tab=summary&timestamp=4.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX is building a platform (ContentOS) that combines AI workflows with expert intervention for content creation and management](https://fathom.video/share/JUt1q6VQCTbYYehU8KF8pr_SDpsxzxft?tab=summary&timestamp=115.0)
  - [The company is experiencing rapid growth, with $10M in the bank, approaching $10M run rate, and strong investor interest](https://fathom.video/share/JUt1q6VQCTbYYehU8KF8pr_SDpsxzxft?tab=summary&timestamp=1636.0)
  - [Nathalie has extensive experience in full-stack content marketing and is excited about the opportunity to focus on editorial work at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/JUt1q6VQCTbYYehU8KF8pr_SDpsxzxft?tab=summary&timestamp=435.0)
  - [GrowthX offers significant growth opportunities for high-performing team members who focus on customer outcomes and company success](https://fathom.video/share/JUt1q6VQCTbYYehU8KF8pr_SDpsxzxft?tab=summary&timestamp=489.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's Vision and Platform

  - [Building ContentOS, a platform combining AI workflows with expert intervention](https://fathom.video/share/JUt1q6VQCTbYYehU8KF8pr_SDpsxzxft?tab=summary&timestamp=115.0)
  - [Solving pain points in content creation and management that existing tools don't address](https://fathom.video/share/JUt1q6VQCTbYYehU8KF8pr_SDpsxzxft?tab=summary&timestamp=194.0)
  - [Developing common cores that could be productized or exposed as APIs in the future](https://fathom.video/share/JUt1q6VQCTbYYehU8KF8pr_SDpsxzxft?tab=summary&timestamp=349.0)
